questions.question: Which of the following describes the 'Accessibility' dimension of food security?

options.A: Food production within the country plus imports
options.B: Food is within reach of every person without any barrier
options.C: Individuals having enough money to buy nutritious food

questions.explanation: Accessibility means food is within reach of every person without any physical or social barrier.

questions.question: Socially, which groups are most prone to food insecurity in India?

options.B: Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and sections of OBCs with low land productivity

questions.explanation: Marginalized social groups often have a poor land-base or very low land productivity, making them vulnerable to food insecurity.

questions.question: Identify the difference between Chronic and Seasonal hunger.

options.A: Chronic is related to crop cycles; Seasonal is related to low income
options.B: Chronic is persistent inadequacy of diet; Seasonal is related to agricultural cycles
options.C: Both are the same in rural areas
options.D: Seasonal hunger only exists in urban areas

questions.explanation: Chronic hunger is a result of persistent poverty, while seasonal hunger occurs during the months when no agricultural work is available.

questions.question: What was the immediate impact of the 1943 Bengal Famine on the population?

options.B: 30 lakh people died due to starvation and epidemics

questions.explanation: The famine led to mass deaths, highlighting the catastrophic failure of food availability and accessibility.

questions.question: The Food Corporation of India (FCI) purchases wheat and rice from farmers in states where there is surplus production at ________.

options.B: Issue Price

questions.explanation: MSP is the government-guaranteed price that provides an incentive to farmers to produce more.

questions.question: What is 'Issue Price' in the context of the Public Distribution System?

options.A: The price at which FCI buys from farmers
options.B: The price at which grain is sold in the open market
options.C: The subsidized price at which grain is sold to the poor through ration shops
options.D: The price of seeds sold to farmers

questions.explanation: The Issue Price is kept lower than the market price to ensure affordability for the poor.

questions.question: Which state has notably used 'Human Resource Development' as a tool for food security and poverty reduction?

options.B: Kerala

questions.explanation: Kerala's focus on education and health has significantly improved the capacity of its people to access food.

questions.question: Under the National Food Security Act (2013), what percentage of the rural population is covered?
questions.explanation: The NFSA 2013 covers 75% of the rural and 50% of the urban population.

questions.question: What is the primary objective of maintaining a 'Buffer Stock'?

options.A: To export grains to other countries for profit
options.B: To distribute grains in deficit areas and during calamities
options.C: To keep prices in the market very high
options.D: To replace the need for farming

questions.explanation: Buffer stock acts as a safety net during periods of food shortage or natural disasters.

questions.question: Targeted Public Distribution System (TPDS) was introduced in 1997 to focus on:

options.B: The poor in all areas (BPL category)

questions.explanation: TPDS shifted the focus from universal coverage to targeting the poor specifically.

questions.question: Which factor does 'Affordability' in food security primarily depend on?

options.B: Purchasing power of an individual

questions.explanation: Affordability implies that individuals have enough money to buy sufficient, safe, and nutritious food.

questions.question: The 'Annapurna Scheme' (APS) targets which specific group?

options.B: Indigent senior citizens

questions.explanation: APS provides 10 kg of food grains free of cost to indigent senior citizens not covered under pensions.

questions.question: Why is the PDS system criticized despite high levels of buffer stocks?

options.A: The grain is too expensive for the government to buy
options.B: Hunger persists despite overflowing granaries and high carrying costs
options.C: Farmers refuse to sell to FCI
options.D: There are too few people below the poverty line

questions.explanation: Inefficiency in distribution and storage leads to the paradox of hunger amidst plenty.

questions.question: In Maharashtra, the Academy of Development Science (ADS) facilitated a network of NGOs for setting up:

options.B: Grain Banks

questions.explanation: ADS grain banks provide food security to tribal communities and serve as a model for decentralized security.

questions.question: What is the result of 'Open-ended Procurement' by the FCI?

options.A: Shortage of grain in the country
options.B: Stocks far exceeding the buffer norms and increasing carrying costs
options.C: Lowering of the MSP for farmers

questions.explanation: Excessive procurement leads to storage problems and a high fiscal burden due to carrying costs.

questions.question: The 'Mid-day Meal Scheme' primarily addresses which two issues simultaneously?

options.B: Nutritional status of children and school attendance

questions.explanation: It provides nutritional support and serves as an incentive for parents to send children to school.

questions.question: Which state's cooperative success led to the 'White Revolution'?

options.B: Gujarat

questions.explanation: Amul in Gujarat revolutionized the dairy industry, ensuring milk security and income for farmers.

questions.question: What happens to the price of food grains during a drought?

options.A: Prices decrease due to low demand
options.B: Prices increase due to shortage of supply
options.C: Prices remain constant due to MSP
options.D: Prices fluctuate based on international trade

questions.explanation: Reduced production creates a supply-demand gap, driving prices up and hurting affordability.

questions.question: The Antyodaya Anna Yojana (AAY) provides how many kilograms of food grains to each eligible family?

options.B: 25 kg

questions.explanation: The quantity was increased from 25 kg to 35 kg in 2002 to further support the poorest of the poor.

questions.question: A 'Subsidy' is a payment made by the government to:

options.A: Increase the market price of goods
options.B: Keep the price of a commodity low for consumers

questions.explanation: Subsidies bridge the gap between production/procurement costs and the price paid by consumers.

questions.question: Which two states were the primary beneficiaries of the Green Revolution in terms of wheat production?

options.B: Punjab and Haryana

questions.explanation: These states had the best irrigation infrastructure to support HYV wheat seeds.

questions.question: One of the malpractices of PDS dealers is 'Black Marketing,' which means:

options.A: Selling grains at the government-fixed issue price
options.B: Diverting grains to the open market to sell at a higher profit
options.D: Giving free grains to poor people

questions.explanation: Black marketing is the illegal sale of subsidized goods in the open market.

questions.question: Why is 'Nutritional Security' considered as important as 'Food Security'?

options.A: To increase the weight of the population
options.B: To ensure that diets are adequate in vitamins and minerals, not just calories
options.D: To reduce the cost of imports

questions.explanation: Calories prevent hunger, but nutrition prevents hidden hunger and long-term health issues.

questions.question: What is the 'Food for Work' (FFW) programme's primary goal?

options.A: To provide office jobs to graduates
options.B: To provide employment and food grains as wages to the poor
options.C: To teach cooking skills to workers
options.D: To increase the export of food

questions.explanation: FFW combines income generation with immediate nutritional support.

questions.question: The 1943 Famine of Bengal was primarily caused by the shortage of which crop?

options.B: Rice

questions.explanation: Rice was the staple food of the region, and its shortage led to mass starvation.
